Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd set aside time to study, maybe 27 hours a week on paper, but I wasn't always mentally present during those hours. Some weeks I'd binge through multiple online courses, other weeks I'd just stare at my notes feeling this heavy sadness that made it hard to focus. I tried to organize things, but life at 29 with all my responsibilities made it difficult to stick to any real schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. I'd go through phases where I was on top of things, then deadlines would pile up and I'd cram everything in at once. Sometimes my mood dictated more than the actual schedule did—on low days, I just couldn't bring myself to engage even when I knew I should. The inconsistency frustrated me because I knew I was capable of more.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about a deadline or this nagging guilt that I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes the gamified elements would pull me back—seeing those progress bars and badges gave me a small dopamine hit that my brain craved on harder days. Other times I logged in just to prove to myself I was still trying, even if I didn't do much once I was there.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became automatic, especially when I got into the rhythm of the gamified tasks. Earning points and unlocking achievements made it feel less like a chore. But those routines never lasted long—something would happen in my life, my mood would dip, and suddenly weeks would pass before I logged in regularly again.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were my lifeline since I learn best through reading and writing. I also found the discussion forums surprisingly helpful because interacting with peers made me feel less isolated. The gamified tasks kept me coming back more than traditional assignments would have—there was something about the immediate feedback that worked for my brain.
Q6: I often skipped the video lectures entirely or watched them at 2x speed without really absorbing anything. They felt passive and my attention would drift, especially when I was already feeling low. Some of the supplementary readings I'd download with good intentions but never actually open. If something felt optional and didn't contribute to my grade or a badge, it usually got ignored.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate progress or results—like completing a gamified task and watching my score go up. I also valued things that connected to real-world applications or sparked genuine curiosity. If something felt like busywork or if I couldn't see the point, my motivation evaporated quickly. At 29, I don't have patience for things that feel meaningless.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford emotionally. Required ones came with pressure and guilt, which sometimes motivated me but often just added to my anxiety. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that would be nice' but then never actually do it because my energy was so limited. Required activities got done because I had to, not because I wanted to.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download PDFs and readings to my tablet and study them offline for hours. I kept extensive handwritten notes that helped me process information better than typing ever could. I also used external websites, YouTube videos, and even some textbooks from the library. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd sit with printed materials spread across my kitchen table, really wrestling with concepts, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd reread my notes, make connections between ideas, even discuss course content with a friend over coffee. The LMS only captured a fraction of the mental work I was actually doing.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF, maybe skim it once online to get the gist, then spend days working through it offline. My highlighted and annotated copies looked completely different from the pristine originals. The LMS would show one quick access, but that material might have been my companion for a whole week of studying.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engaging felt impossible. My sadness would get heavy, and even simple tasks felt overwhelming. Sometimes it was external stuff—work stress, family obligations, just the weight of being a 29-year-old trying to juggle everything. My attendance dropped significantly during those times, even though I was technically enrolled in six online courses.
Q13: Fatigue was a constant companion, honestly. Between work, life responsibilities, and managing my mental state, I often came to the LMS already depleted. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes just close the tab rather than push through because I didn't have the emotional reserves. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS often got whatever scraps of energy I had left.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I'd watch discussion posts scroll by without really reading them, or click through pages just to feel like I was doing something. Those sessions felt hollow—present in body but absent in spirit.
Q15: I postponed a lot of things, especially when my anxiety spiked about not understanding something. It was easier to avoid than to confront my confusion and feel stupid. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow would come and the sadness would still be there. It became a cycle of avoidance that I couldn't easily break.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my savior. They forced me to engage when nothing else could, but they also caused panic that made my anxiety worse. I'd often complete assignments right before they were due, spending intense bursts of energy rather than steady effort. My high assignment completion rate is basically a testament to deadline pressure, not good planning.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would boost my mood temporarily and make me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even when constructive, could send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. The gamified feedback was easier to handle—points and badges felt less personal than written comments about my work.
Q18: The course structure mattered a lot. When things were clearly laid out with obvious pathways, I could navigate even on bad days. But when the structure was confusing or required too much self-direction, I'd get lost and frustrated. The gamified elements helped because they created a clear progression that my scattered brain could follow.
Q19: The gamified tasks genuinely made engagement easier—they broke things into manageable chunks and gave me small wins. But the sheer volume of six online courses made everything harder. Some design choices, like burying important information in multiple clicks, frustrated me when I was already low on energy. Clear, simple interfaces helped; cluttered ones pushed me away.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on certain days—logging in, clicking around, maybe participating in discussions, but not having the mental clarity to actually produce work. Sometimes you're going through the motions hoping engagement will spark something, but the actual output doesn't materialize. It could also be someone gathering information for a big submission later.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam scores weren't great, but I passed, and a lot of my learning happened offline where no one could track it. Someone might download everything they need early, study intensively offline, and only appear briefly to submit work. The LMS doesn't capture the hours spent with printed notes or the conversations with study partners.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how often you engage, what you click on. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and what's happening in your head. They can't see the offline studying, the emotional struggles, or the difference between mindless clicking and genuine learning. They capture behavior but not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and feeling mentally present while doing it. It's not just showing up or completing tasks—it's when the material connects with something in me and I want to understand it deeply. Real engagement happens when I'm curious, not just compliant. Unfortunately, that feeling has been harder to access lately.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my emotional state affected everything—the sadness that made some weeks nearly impossible, the anxiety that led to avoidance. It wouldn't capture the extensive offline studying I did, the peer conversations, or the genuine effort I put in despite struggling. It also can't show that I'm a 29-year-old with a full life outside academics, doing my best with limited emotional resources.
